title: Understanding dividing fractions by fractions
topic_path: Using an algorithm to divide fractions

assessment_items.question_content:
**How many times does $\dfrac23$ go into $\dfrac83$ ?**  
 [[☃ input-number 1]]

**Which division problem can we use to get this answer?**
[[☃ radio 1]]

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0b4dc8dd2969063c62588c8e049d08f9.png)
hints:
Look at the length of the segment that measures $\dfrac{2}{3}$ .  

The segment measuring $\dfrac{8}{3}$ could be divided into $4$ pieces with the same length as the $\dfrac{2}{3}$ segment.

So, $\dfrac{2}{3}$ goes into $\dfrac{8}{3}$ exactly $4$ times.


We can use the division problem  $\dfrac83\div\dfrac23$ to get this answer.

assessment_items.question_content:
**A segment of length $2$ is made up of how many segments of length $\dfrac13$ ?**  
 [[☃ input-number 1]]

**Which division problem can we use to get this answer?**
[[☃ radio 1]]


![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6b1a436eace5f5bf2a0f8dfbeda4951e.png)
hints:
Look at the length of the segment that measures $\dfrac{1}{3}$ .  

The segment measuring $2 = \dfrac{6}{3}$ could be divided into $6$ pieces with the same length as   the $\dfrac{1}{3}$ segment.

So, a segment of length $2$ is made up of $6$ segments of length $\dfrac{1}{3}$ . 

We can use the division problem  $2\div\dfrac13$ to get this answer.

The segment measuring $\dfrac{3}{2} = \dfrac{12}{8}$ could be divided into $4$ pieces with the same length as   the $\dfrac{3}{8}$ segment.

So, $\dfrac{3}{8}$ goes into $\dfrac{3}{2}$ exactly $4$ times.

We can use the division problem  $\dfrac32\div\dfrac38$ to get this answer.

So, a segment of length $\dfrac{9}{5}$ is made up of $3$ segments of length $\dfrac{3}{5}$ .


We can use the division problem $\dfrac95\div\dfrac35$ to get this answer.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How many times does $\dfrac{1}{10}$ go into $\dfrac{3}{2}$ ?**  
 [[☃ input-number 1]]

**Which division problem can we use to get this answer?**
[[☃ radio 1]]


![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/07dd5a272db69d136d2e0685c8b97fca.png)
hints:
Look at the length of the segment that measures $\dfrac{1}{10}$ .  

The segment measuring $\dfrac{3}{2} = \dfrac{15}{10}$ could be divided into $15$ pieces with the same length as   the $\dfrac{1}{10}$ segment.

So, $\dfrac{1}{10}$ goes into $\dfrac{3}{2}$ exactly $15$ times.

We can use the division problem  $\dfrac{3}{2}\div\dfrac{1}{10}$ to get this answer.

We can use the division problem  $\dfrac{8}{5}\div\dfrac{4}{10}$ to get this answer.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How many times does $\dfrac14$ go into $\dfrac{5}{2}$ ?**  
 [[☃ input-number 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d681f475e9166a20954c019c1f56a22c.png)
hints:
Look at the length of the segment that measures $\dfrac{1}{4}$ .  

The segment measuring $\dfrac{5}{2} = \dfrac{10}{4}$ could be divided into $10$ pieces with the same length as   the $\dfrac{1}{4}$ segment.

So, $\dfrac{1}{4}$ goes into $\dfrac{5}{2}$ exactly $10$ times.


We can use the division problem $\dfrac{5}2\div\dfrac14$ to get this answer.

So, $\dfrac{3}{4}$ goes into $\dfrac{3}{2}$ exactly $2$ times.


We can use the division problem $\dfrac{3}2\div\dfrac34$ to get this answer.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select whether the quotient for each fraction division expression is less than one or greater than/equal to one.**  

[[☃ categorizer 1]]
hints:
When $\dfrac{7}{10}$ is divided by a number that is *smaller* than itself, the quotient will be *greater than one* since more than one of the smaller fraction pieces will fit into $\dfrac{7}{10}$ .  

When $\dfrac{7}{10}$ is divided by a number that is *larger* than itself, the quotient will be *less than one*, since the piece is too large to fit into $\dfrac{7}{10}$  even once.

Let's look at $\dfrac{7}{10}\div\dfrac{14}{10}$ .  

$\dfrac{14}{10}$ is *larger than* $\dfrac{7}{10}$ , so $\dfrac{14}{10}$ will not fit into $\dfrac{7}{10}$ even once.    

$\dfrac{7}{10}\div\dfrac{14}{10}$ $<1$.


Let's look at $\dfrac{7}{10}\div\dfrac{14}{20}$ .  

When we convert $\dfrac{7}{10}$  to $\dfrac{14}{20}$ to compare the fractions, we see that the fractions are equal.

$\dfrac{14}{20}$ goes into $\dfrac{7}{10}$ exactly once.

So, $\dfrac{7}{10}\div\dfrac{14}{20}$ $\geq$ $1$.

Let's look at $\dfrac{7}{10}\div\dfrac{1}{2}$ .   

We know that $\dfrac{1}{2}$ is *smaller than* $\dfrac{7}{10}$ .
  
Therefore, at least one $\dfrac{1}{2}$ piece fits into $\dfrac{7}{10}$ . 

So, $\dfrac{7}{10}\div\dfrac{1}{2}$ $\geq$ $1$.
Let's look at $\dfrac{7}{10}\div\dfrac{4}{5}$ .  We convert $\dfrac{4}{5}$ to $\dfrac{8}{10}$ to compare the fractions.
  
Not even one $\dfrac{4}{5}$ piece fits into $\dfrac{7}{10}$ , since $\dfrac{4}{5}$ is *larger than* $\dfrac{7}{10}$ .

So, $\dfrac{7}{10}\div\dfrac{4}{5}$ $<1$.

Quotient $<1$ | Quotient $\geq$ $1$
:-: | :-: 
$\dfrac{7}{10}\div\dfrac{14}{10}$ | $\dfrac{7}{10}\div\dfrac{14}{20}$
$\dfrac{7}{10}\div\dfrac{4}{5}$ | $\dfrac{7}{10}\div\dfrac{1}{2}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select whether the quotient for each fraction division expression is less than one or greater than/equal to one.**  

[[☃ categorizer 1]]
hints:
When $\dfrac{23}{20}$ is divided by a number that is *smaller* than itself, the quotient will be *greater than one* since more than one of the smaller fraction pieces will fit into $\dfrac{23}{20}$ .  

When $\dfrac{23}{20}$ is divided by a number that is *larger* than itself, the quotient will be *less than one*, since the piece is too large to fit into $\dfrac{23}{20}$  even once.

Let's think of $\dfrac{23}{20}$ as *one whole and a bit more* to make comparisons.



$\dfrac{13}{10}$ , $\dfrac{67}{40}$ , $\dfrac{53}{50}$ , and $\dfrac{21}{20}$ are each more than one whole, 

but are they larger than $\dfrac{23}{20}$$$ ?



$\dfrac{13}{10}$ and $\dfrac{67}{40}$ are *larger than* $\dfrac{23}{20}$ , so they will not fit into $\dfrac{23}{20}$ even once.    

$\dfrac{53}{50}$ and $\dfrac{21}{20}$ are *smaller* than $\dfrac{23}{20}$ and so will fit into $\dfrac{23}{20}$ at least once.  

$\dfrac{89}{90}$ is less than one whole and also fits into $\dfrac{23}{20}$ at least once.
Quotient $<1$ | Quotient $\geq$ $1$
:-: | :-: 
$\dfrac{23}{20}\div\dfrac{13}{10}$ | $\dfrac{23}{20}\div\dfrac{53}{50}$
$\dfrac{23}{20}\div\dfrac{67}{40}$| $\dfrac{23}{20}\div\dfrac{89}{90}$
|$\dfrac{23}{20}\div\dfrac{21}{20}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select whether the quotient for each fraction division expression is less than one or greater than/equal to one.**  

[[☃ categorizer 1]]
hints:
When $\dfrac45$ is divided by a number that is *smaller* than itself, the quotient will be *greater than one* since more than one of the smaller fraction pieces will fit into $\dfrac45$ .  

When $\dfrac45$ is divided by a number that is *larger* than itself, the quotient will be *less than one*, since the piece is too large to fit into $\dfrac45$  even once.

Let's look at $\dfrac45\div\dfrac{9}{10}$ .  We convert $\dfrac45$  to $\dfrac{8}{10}$ in order to compare the fractions.

$\dfrac{9}{10}$ is *larger than* $\dfrac{8}{10}$ , so $\dfrac{9}{10}$ will not fit into $\dfrac{8}{10}$ even once.    

So, $\dfrac45\div\dfrac{9}{10}$ $<1$.


Let's look at $\dfrac45\div\dfrac{3}{10}$ .  We convert $\dfrac45$  to $\dfrac{8}{10}$ in order to compare the fractions.

$\dfrac{3}{10}$ is *smaller than* $\dfrac{8}{10}$ , so $\dfrac{3}{10}$ will fit into $\dfrac{8}{10}$ at least once.    

So, $\dfrac45\div\dfrac{3}{10}$ $\geq$ $1$.

Let's look at $\dfrac45\div\dfrac{2}{5}$ .  
  
At least one $\dfrac{2}{5}$ piece fits into $\dfrac45$ , since $\dfrac{2}{5}$ is *smaller than* $\dfrac45$ .  

In fact, exactly $2$ pieces of $\dfrac{2}{5}$ fit into $\dfrac45$ .

So, $\dfrac45\div\dfrac{2}{5}$ $\geq$ $1$.
Let's look at $\dfrac45\div\dfrac{4}{9}$ .  
  
At least one $\dfrac{4}{9}$ piece fits into $\dfrac45$ , since $\dfrac{4}{9}$ is *smaller than* $\dfrac45$ .

So, $\dfrac45\div\dfrac{4}{9}$ $\geq$ $1$ .

Let's look at $\dfrac45\div\dfrac{4}{3}$ .  
  
Not even one $\dfrac{4}{3}$ piece fits into $\dfrac45$ , since $\dfrac{4}{3}$ is *larger than* $\dfrac45$ .   

So, $\dfrac45\div\dfrac{4}{3}$ $<1$.
Quotient $<1$ | Quotient $\geq$ $1$
:-: | :-: 
$\dfrac45\div\dfrac{9}{10}$ | $\dfrac45\div\dfrac{3}{10}$ 
$\dfrac45\div\dfrac{4}{3}$ | $\dfrac45\div\dfrac{2}{5}$ 
 | $\dfrac45\div\dfrac{4}{9}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select whether the quotient for each fraction division expression is less than one or greater than/equal to one.**  

[[☃ categorizer 1]]
hints:
When $\dfrac{1}{2}$ is divided by a number that is *smaller* than itself, the quotient will be *greater than one*, since more than one of the smaller fraction pieces will fit into $\dfrac{1}{2}$ .  

When $\dfrac{1}{2}$ is divided by a number that is *larger* than itself, the quotient will be *less than one*, since the piece is too large to fit into $\dfrac{1}{2}$  even once.

Let's look at $\dfrac{1}{2}\div\dfrac{53}{100}$ .  

$\dfrac{53}{100}$ is *larger than* $\dfrac{1}{2}$ , so $\dfrac{53}{100}$ will not fit into $\dfrac{1}{2}$ even once.    

$\dfrac{1}{2}\div\dfrac{53}{100}$ $<1$.


The fraction $\dfrac{13}{22}$ is also *larger than* $\dfrac{1}{2}$ , so it will fit into $\dfrac{1}{2}$ less than once.

The fractions $\dfrac{24}{50}$ and $\dfrac{47}{100}$ are both *smaller* than $\dfrac{1}{2}$ and so will fit into $\dfrac{1}{2}$ at least once.
Quotient $<1$ | Quotient $\geq$ $1$
:-: | :-: 
$\dfrac{1}{2}\div\dfrac{53}{100}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}\div\dfrac{24}{50}$
$\dfrac{1}{2}\div\dfrac{13}{22}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}\div\dfrac{47}{100}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select whether the quotient for each fraction division expression is less than one or greater than/equal to one.**  

[[☃ categorizer 1]]
hints:
When $\dfrac{1}{2}$ is divided by a number that is *smaller* than itself, the quotient will be *greater than one*, since more than one of the smaller fraction pieces will fit into $\dfrac{1}{2}$ .  

When $\dfrac{1}{2}$ is divided by a number that is *larger* than itself, the quotient will be *less than one*, since the piece is too large to fit into $\dfrac{1}{2}$  even once.

Let's look at $\dfrac{1}{2}\div\dfrac{2}{3}$ .  

$\dfrac{2}{3}$ is *larger than* $\dfrac{1}{2}$ , so $\dfrac{2}{3}$ will not fit into $\dfrac{1}{2}$ even once.    

$\dfrac{1}{2}\div\dfrac{2}{3}$ $<1$.


The fractions $\dfrac{1}{3}$ , $\dfrac{1}{8}$ , and $\dfrac{2}{9}$ are all *smaller* than $\dfrac{1}{2}$ .  

That means they each will fit into $\dfrac{1}{2}$ at least once.

Quotient $<1$ | Quotient $\geq$ $1$
:-: | :-: 
$\dfrac{1}{2}\div\dfrac{2}{3}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}\div\dfrac{1}{3}$
 | $\dfrac{1}{2}\div\dfrac{1}{8}$
| $\dfrac{1}{2}\div\dfrac{2}{9}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select whether the quotient for each fraction division expression is less than one or greater than/equal to one.**  

[[☃ categorizer 1]]
hints:
When $\dfrac{3}{2}$ is divided by a number that is *smaller* than itself, the quotient will be *greater than one* since more than one of the smaller fraction pieces will fit into $\dfrac{3}{2}$ .  

When $\dfrac{3}{2}$ is divided by a number that is *larger* than itself, the quotient will be *less than one*, since the piece is too large to fit into $\dfrac{3}{2}$  even once.

Let's convert $\dfrac{3}{2}$ to $\dfrac{75}{50}$ to make the comparisons.



$\dfrac{81}{50}$ is *larger than* $\dfrac{75}{50}$ , so $\dfrac{81}{50}$ will not fit into $\dfrac{3}{2}$ even once.    

The fractions $\dfrac{25}{50}$ ,  $\dfrac{31}{50}$ , and $\dfrac{61}{50}$ are all *smaller* than $\dfrac{75}{50}$ and so will fit into $\dfrac{3}{2}$ at least once.  

$\dfrac{75}{50}$ is equal to $\dfrac{3}{2}$ and fits in exactly once.
Quotient $<1$ | Quotient $\geq$ $1$
:-: | :-: 
$\dfrac{3}{2}\div\dfrac{81}{50}$ | $\dfrac{3}{2}\div\dfrac{25}{50}$
| $\dfrac{3}{2}\div\dfrac{31}{50}$
|$\dfrac{3}{2}\div\dfrac{61}{50}$
|$\dfrac{3}{2}\div\dfrac{75}{50}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select whether the quotient for each fraction division expression is less than one or greater than/equal to one.**  

[[☃ categorizer 1]]
hints:
When $\dfrac{1}{4}$ is divided by a number that is *smaller* than itself, the quotient will be *greater than one*, since more than one of the smaller fraction pieces will fit into $\dfrac{1}{4}$ .  

When $\dfrac{1}{4}$ is divided by a number that is *larger* than itself, the quotient will be *less than one*, since the piece is too large to fit into $\dfrac{1}{4}$  even once.

Let's look at $\dfrac{1}{4}\div\dfrac{2}{3}$ .  

$\dfrac{2}{3}$ is *larger than* $\dfrac{1}{4}$ , so $\dfrac{2}{3}$ will not fit into $\dfrac{1}{4}$ even once.    

$\dfrac{1}{4}\div\dfrac{2}{3}$ $<1$.

The fractions $\dfrac{1}{3}$ and $\dfrac{2}{5}$ are also both *larger than* $\dfrac{1}{4}$ , so they will fit into $\dfrac{1}{4}$ less than once.

The fraction $\dfrac{1}{5}$ is *smaller* than $\dfrac{1}{4}$ and so will fit into $\dfrac{1}{4}$ at least once.

Quotient $<1$ | Quotient $\geq$ $1$
:-: | :-: 
$\dfrac{1}{4}\div\dfrac{2}{3}$ |$\dfrac{1}{4}\div\dfrac{1}{5}$
 $\dfrac{1}{4}\div\dfrac{1}{3}$ | 
$\dfrac{1}{4}\div\dfrac{2}{5}$|

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select whether the quotient for each fraction division expression is less than one or greater than/equal to one.**  

[[☃ categorizer 1]]
hints:
When $\dfrac{1}{3}$ is divided by a number that is *smaller* than itself, the quotient will be *greater than one*, since more than one of the smaller fraction pieces will fit into $\dfrac{1}{3}$ .  

When $\dfrac{1}{3}$ is divided by a number that is *larger* than itself, the quotient will be *less than one*, since the piece is too large to fit into $\dfrac{1}{3}$  even once.

Let's look at $\dfrac{1}{3}\div\dfrac{3}{4}$ .  

$\dfrac{3}{4}$ is *larger than* $\dfrac{1}{3}$ , so $\dfrac{3}{4}$ will not fit into $\dfrac{1}{3}$ even once.    

$\dfrac{1}{3}\div\dfrac{3}{4}$ $<1$.

The fractions $\dfrac{2}{3}$ and $\dfrac{3}{5}$ are also both *larger than* $\dfrac{1}{3}$ , so they will fit into $\dfrac{1}{3}$ less than once.

The fraction $\dfrac{1}{7}$ is *smaller* than $\dfrac{1}{3}$ and so will fit into $\dfrac{1}{3}$ at least once.

Quotient $<1$ | Quotient $\geq$ $1$
:-: | :-: 
$\dfrac{1}{3}\div\dfrac{3}{4}$ |$\dfrac{1}{3}\div\dfrac{1}{7}$
 $\dfrac{1}{3}\div\dfrac{2}{3}$ | 
$\dfrac{1}{3}\div\dfrac{3}{5}$|

assessment_items.question_content:
**Fill in the blank to make the equation true:**

$\dfrac47\times\dfrac72$ $=$ $\dfrac47\div$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Dividing fractions is the same as inverting (or "flipping") and multiplying. 

Multiplying $\dfrac{4}{7}$ by $\dfrac{7}{2}$ is the same as dividing  $\dfrac{4}{7}$ by $\dfrac{2}{7}$ . 

The correct answer is $\dfrac{2}{7}$ .

assessment_items.question_content:
**Fill in the blank to make the equation true:**

$\dfrac{9}{10}\times\dfrac{10}{3}$ $=$ $\dfrac{9}{10}\div$ [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Dividing fractions is the same as inverting (or "flipping") and multiplying. 

Multiplying $\dfrac{9}{10}$ by $\dfrac{10}{3}$ is the same as dividing  $\dfrac{9}{10}$ by $\dfrac{3}{10}$ . 

The correct answer is $\dfrac{3}{10}$ .
